title: Safely managed drinking water access is determined by the lowest value of the three component
subtitle: People with access to water free from contamination, water accessible on premises, or water available when needed (% of people using improved water sources)
source: WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP) for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene, JMP Global Database.
visdescription: Three-column charts show the components of safely managed drinking water among people using improved water sources. The left column represents water that is free from contamination; the middle column, water that is accessible on premises; and the right column, water that is available when needed. The first scene shows data for Mongolia; the second, for Nepal.

scenes.text: For example, [emphasis: in Mongolia], 84 percent of individuals with improved water sources have water available when needed, and 82 percent have access to uncontaminated water. But [emphasis: only 43 percent ]have improved water sources at home, resulting in an estimate of [emphasis: 43 percent][emphasis: ]of people with safely managed drinking water access. So, for Mongolia, accessibility is the limiting factor.

Conversely, [emphasis: in Nepal], accessibility and availabilityare relatively high (around 80 percent each), but [emphasis: only 16 percent have access to ][emphasis: uncontaminated water]. As a result, only around [emphasis: 1][emphasis: 6 percent] have access to safely managed drinking water. So, in Nepal, water quality (free from contamination)is the limiting factor in access to safely managed drinking water.
